coredata.dc:title: Metal-free catalysis for efficient reduction of 4-nitrophenol by graphene quantum dots controlled by the surface trapped water molecules
coredata.dc:creator: Sinha, Riya
Purkayastha, Pradipta
coredata.dc:description: We report the synthesis of environment-friendly and water-soluble metal-free functional carbon materials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), applied as catalysts for efficient reduction of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) to 4-aminophenol (4-AP) in aqueous solution at room temperature and ambient condition. The one-pot and easily synthesized GQDs show excellent variation in catalytic property in reducing 4-NP. While the unprotected GQDs demonstrate relatively slow conversion of 4-NP to 4-AP, the protein-protected and thiolate-coated GQDs show enhancement in the reduction rate by two orders of magnitude. The heterogeneous catalytic activity by the three variants of GQDs appears to depend on the extent of core surface-trapped water molecules.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon materials
Graphene quantum dot
Catalyst
Functional
Surface water
Adsorption

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HR-TEM images of (a) GQD, (b) GSH-GQD and (c) BSA-GQD.

Changes in the absorption spectra showing reduction of 4-NP by NaBH4 in the presence of (a) GQDs, (b) GSH-GQDs, and (c) BSA-GQDs in aqueous buffer at 25 °C; (d) comparison of the rates of reduction of 4-NP in the three cases.
Comparison of the rate of catalytic reduction of 4-NP to 4-AP from the present work with the literature reports on metal-free graphene-based catalysts.

head.ce:title: Metal-free catalysis for efficient reduction of 4-nitrophenol by graphene quantum dots controlled by the surface trapped water molecules

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Sciences and Center for Advanced Functional Materials, Indian Institute of Science Education and Research (IISER) Kolkata, Mohanpur 741246, WB, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Sciences and Center for Advanced Functional Materials
Indian Institute of Science Education and Research (IISER) Kolkata

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Sciences and Center for Advanced Functional Materials, Indian Institute of Science Education and Research (IISER) Kolkata, Mohanpur 741246, WB, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report the synthesis of environment-friendly and water-soluble metal-free functional carbon materials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), applied as catalysts for efficient reduction of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) to 4-aminophenol (4-AP) in aqueous solution at room temperature and ambient condition. The one-pot and easily synthesized GQDs show excellent variation in catalytic property in reducing 4-NP. While the unprotected GQDs demonstrate relatively slow conversion of 4-NP to 4-AP, the protein-protected and thiolate-coated GQDs show enhancement in the reduction rate by two orders of magnitude. The heterogeneous catalytic activity by the three variants of GQDs appears to depend on the extent of core surface-trapped water molecules.

ce:section.ce:para: 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) is a notorious nitroaromatic pollutant in industrial and agricultural sewage severely affecting the aquatic life [1]. Hence, catalytic reduction of 4-NP to 4-aminophenol (4-AP) using metal-based catalysts, in the presence of sodium borohydride (NaBH4) as reducing agent, has been widely practiced [2–4]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) evolved as functional carbon materials over time as useful bio-friendly zero-dimensional substances with plenty of applications including heterogeneous catalysis [5–7]. Metal-free environment benign GQDs [8–10] have not been used to catalytically reduce 4-NP to 4-AP in the aqueous medium. Hence, we synthesized glutathione (GSH) and bovine serum albumin (BSA) protected GQDs by simple synthetic methods and observed that the variety of surface coatings on the GQDs (GSH and BSA) controlled the optimal presence of water at the GQD interface without imparting toxicity that helped in enhancing the capability of catalytic reduction of 4-NP in the aqueous environment.

The GQDs, GSH-GQDs and BSA-GQDs were synthesized from citric acid following previous reports [11–13]. The sizes of the crystalline (Fig. 1 a, b and c) and well-dispersed (Fig. S1a, c and e) GQD variants were around 3–6 nm (Figs. S1b, d and f) with interplanar spacing of around 0.21 nm and zeta potentials of −11.5, −15 and −31.3 mV, respectively (Fig. S2).

The FTIR signals of the GQDs (Fig. S3a) at 3440 cm−1 and 1640 cm−1 indicated the presence of the O–H and C = O groups belonging to the –COOH moiety. Amide C = O stretching signal at 1717 cm−1 was observed for GSH-GQD and BSA-GQD. A characteristic S-H stretching vibration band at 2594 cm−1 for GSH-GQD confirmed anchoring of GSH to the GQD surface [14]. The 1234 cm−1 signal for BSA-GQD was due to the amide III of BSA. The UV–visible spectrum of the unprotected GQDs showed a broad shoulder and a peak at ∼280 and ∼358 nm due to the π − π* and n − π* transitions in the aromatic rings and the CO bonds of the –COOH groups, respectively (Fig. S3b) [7]. The n − π* bands for the GSH-GQD and BSA-GQD appear at 336 and 344 nm, respectively. The bare GQDs show strong blue fluorescence at 460 nm on excitation at 360 nm (Fig. S3c). The emission maximum is practically independent of the excitation wavelength signifying monodispersity (Figs. S4a and S4b) [7]. These GQD variants are equally stable over an extensive time on continuous exposure to light (Fig. S5).

Shifts in the emission maxima indicate that the protected GQD-cores experience less micropolarity compared to the bare ones. From this conjecture, it is fair enough to speculate that surface of the bare GQDs is encapsulated with higher number of water molecules than those of the GSH- and BSA-protected ones [14]. Because of the presence of oxygen containing surface functionalities, the GQDs are hydrophilic and hence the water molecules get adsorbed at the active sites [14]. More importantly, the surface adsorbed water molecules remarkably enhance the rate of heterocatalysis [15,16]. Very high concentration of water adversely affects the process by blocking the active sites [15]. It is known that water molecules can dissociate on the nanoparticle surface with very small activation energy of dissociation (23 kJ/mol ) [16,17]. The GQD variants cater 62.16 (460 nm), 66.49 (430 nm) and 64.40 (444 nm) kcal/mol energy at the surface, which can sufficiently dissociate water and favor catalysis. The solvent-exposed bare GQD surfaces possess plenty of adsorbed water molecules that would likely block most of the surface sites available for catalysis [15]. Whereas, the surface of the GQDs covered with BSA will contain lesser number of water molecules. However, greater packing of GSH on the GQD surface would allow decrease water adsorption.

The ultrasmall GQDs furnish extensive surface sites for the adsorption of 4-NP and the reductant NaBH4. An aqueous pale-yellow solution of 4-NP (10 μM) shows strong absorption at 317 nm. Addition of excess NaBH4 (1 mM) produces 4-nitrophenolate that shows an intense absorption band at 400 nm in expense of the 317 nm band and imparts bright yellow color to the solution. NaBH4 is incapable to convert 4-nitophenolate to 4-AP without any catalyst. Addition of a small amount of the bare GQDs (10 μg/mL) to the solution slowly lowered the 400 nm band (Fig. 2 a) and the color of the solution faded with the evolution of the 300 nm band for 4-AP. Much faster conversion of 4-NP to 4-AP was observed on addition of GSH-GQD and BSA-GQD (Figs. 2b and 2c). The reductant, NaBH4, was taken in large excess as compared to 4-NP (100:1) and hence its concentration is considered to remain unchanged during the course of reaction. The kinetic equation for the pseudo-unimolecular reduction reaction can be represented by: ln(A0/At) = kt, where A0 is the initial absorbance of 4-nitrophenolate at 400 nm and At is its absorbance at time t, and k is the apparent rate constant. Plots of ln(A0/At) vs. t in presence of the GQD-variants were found to be linear (Fig. 2d). The apparent rate constants in each case, obtained from the respective slopes of the plots, are 3.95 × 10−5, 3.13 × 10−3, and 1.26 × 10−3 (s−1) in presence of the GQDs, GSH-GQDs and BSA-GQDs, respectively.

The photoexcited GQDs act as electron donor and the sacrificial reductant, NaBH4, fills up the holes in the ground electronic state. Photochemical dissociation of water and the reaction between NaBH4 and H2O generate molecular hydrogens that adsorb on the GQD surface and reduce 4-NP [10]. The reduction reaction is heavily dependent on the nature of the coating that controls the penetration of water. The rate constants are comparable with the other metal-free graphene-based catalysts (Table 1 ).

Bare, GSH- and BSA-protected GQDs were designed to catalytically reduce 4-NP at 25 °C. The GSH and BSA protections are different in terms of packing over the GQDs and hence on the degree of water permeation. Noting that presence of an optimum amount of water molecules on the surface of the core material considerably enhances the catalytic efficiency, we probed the reduction of 4-NP to 4-AP using three GQD variants.

Riya Sinha: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft. Pradipta Purkayastha: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work is financially supported by the Scientific and Engineering Research Board (SERB) (CRG/2018/000555). RS acknowledges IISER Kolkata for her fellowship.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2022.132054.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metal-Free Catalytic reduction of 4-nitrophenol to 4-aminophenol by N-doped graphene
Energy Environ. Sci.
